title: Anabolic steroid abuse: exogenous testosterone
vignette: A 24-year-old man is brought to the office by his wife for evaluation of behavioral changes. The wife reports that he has been increasingly irritable over the last several months and becomes angry for no clear reason. He was recently involved in a fistfight at work. She also adds that they have been unsuccessful in their attempts to conceive over the past year. The patient states that he is "fine" and that nothing is wrong with him. He drinks 5 or 6 beers a week and smoked marijuana regularly when he was in college. He is otherwise healthy and exercises frequently. Temperature is 37 C (98.6 F), blood pressure is 148/92 mm Hg, and pulse is 92/min. On physical examination, heart and lung sounds are normal. There is palpable tissue under his nipples bilaterally, and he has a receding hairline. The patient appears annoyed during the evaluation and provides short responses to questions. Use of which of the following substances is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

clues.text: increasingly irritable over the last several months and becomes angry for no clear reason
clues.note: New, uncharacteristic aggression and irritability ('roid rage') in a young man is a behavioral hallmark of anabolic-androgenic steroid use, which alters mood and impulse control.

clues.text: unsuccessful in their attempts to conceive over the past year
clues.note: Infertility is a key clue: exogenous androgens suppress the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis (low LH/FSH), causing testicular atrophy and decreased spermatogenesis - hence subfertility despite a virilized appearance.
Frequent exercise/gym context is the demographic setup for anabolic steroid use, which is taken to enhance muscle mass and athletic performance.

clues.text: palpable tissue under his nipples bilaterally
clues.note: Bilateral subareolar breast tissue is gynecomastia, caused by aromatization of excess testosterone into estradiol - a classic sign of androgen excess from anabolic steroids.
Male-pattern hair loss results from conversion of testosterone to dihydrotestosterone (DHT), which miniaturizes scalp follicles - another androgenic sign tying the picture to exogenous testosterone.

whatMatters: Strip the story to: a young, frequently-exercising man with new aggression, INFERTILITY, GYNECOMASTIA, and male-pattern balding (plus hypertension). That cluster is anabolic-androgenic steroid abuse. Exogenous testosterone suppresses the HPG axis (infertility/testicular atrophy), aromatizes to estradiol (gynecomastia), and converts to DHT (balding). Answer D.
strategy: Cluster the signs of EXOGENOUS ANDROGEN excess in a young athletic man: behavioral aggression + gynecomastia + testicular atrophy/infertility + acne/balding + hypertension. That constellation is anabolic-androgenic steroid (testosterone) abuse. Don't be sidetracked by recreational drugs that fail to explain the gynecomastia and infertility.

eliminations.label: amphetamine - no gynecomastia/infertility
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: a stimulant misused for alertness and performance; it can cause hypertension, irritability, and aggression. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it does not cause gynecomastia or infertility/testicular atrophy, which specifically point to androgen excess. ✅ Correct if: stimulant intoxication without the androgenic signs.

eliminations.label: growth hormone - acromegalic, not androgenic
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: growth hormone is misused by athletes and can cause acromegalic features, hypertension, and glucose intolerance. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: GH does not cause gynecomastia or the androgenic infertility/balding pattern. ✅ Correct if: enlarging hands/jaw and soft-tissue overgrowth.

eliminations.label: phencyclidine (PCP) - violence but no endocrine signs
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: a dissociative causing violent/agitated behavior, nystagmus, and analgesia. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: PCP explains aggression but not gynecomastia, infertility, or balding. ✅ Correct if: a violent patient with vertical/horizontal nystagmus and analgesia.

eliminations.label: tetrahydrocannabinol (THC) - doesn't fit the full picture (your pick)
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: cannabis; it can cause hypertension and, with chronic use, reduced spermatogenesis. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it does not explain gynecomastia, male-pattern balding, and marked aggression together as well as anabolic steroids do, and his marijuana use was remote (college). The trap is the mentioned drug history. ✅ Correct if: acute cannabis effects (conjunctival injection, increased appetite) were the focus.

reasoning: Young athletic man with new aggression, infertility, gynecomastia, receding hairline, and hypertension
This is the constellation of anabolic-androgenic steroid (testosterone) abuse
Exogenous testosterone suppresses LH/FSH (infertility), aromatizes to estradiol (gynecomastia), and converts to DHT (balding)
Most likely substance -> testosterone -> answer D
mechanism: Anabolic-androgenic steroids (testosterone, stanozolol, nandrolone) are misused to boost muscle mass and performance. Exogenous androgen suppresses hypothalamic-pituitary gonadotropins (LH/FSH), causing testicular atrophy, decreased spermatogenesis, and INFERTILITY. Excess testosterone is AROMATIZED to estradiol, producing GYNECOMASTIA, and is converted by 5-alpha-reductase to DHT, causing male-pattern hair loss and acne. Other effects include aggression/mood changes ('roid rage'), hypertension, polycythemia, and adverse lipid profiles. Patients often deny use, so a high index of suspicion is needed.

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Endo/Pharm > androgens and anabolic steroid abuse: HPG suppression (infertility, testicular atrophy), aromatization to estradiol (gynecomastia), DHT (balding/acne), behavioral and cardiovascular effects.

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: Cluster the findings rather than chasing one symptom: aggression + gynecomastia + infertility + balding + hypertension in a gym-goer = anabolic steroids. Reject drugs that explain only the mood change.

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on anabolic-androgenic steroids, gynecomastia, aromatization, dihydrotestosterone (DHT), HPG axis suppression, spermatogenesis? Tell me which and I'll explain it.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: THC (E) is the trap because his history mentions marijuana - but it doesn't explain gynecomastia, balding, and infertility together. Weight the specific androgenic signs over the named recreational drug.

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: Bank the picture: muscles up, testes down, breast tissue, receding hairline, short temper = anabolic steroids (testosterone).

keywords.front: Young athlete + aggression + gynecomastia + infertility + balding

firstAid.detail: Endo/Pharm > androgens: HPG suppression, aromatization (gynecomastia), DHT (balding), behavioral and cardiovascular effects.
firstAid.location: Endo/Pharm > androgens: HPG suppression, aromatization (gynecomastia), DHT (balding), behavioral and cardiovascular effects.

firstAid.detail: Psych/Pharm > amphetamine, PCP, cannabis (THC) intoxication features; growth hormone misuse (acromegalic features).
firstAid.location: Psych/Pharm > amphetamine, PCP, cannabis (THC) intoxication features; growth hormone misuse (acromegalic features).

memoryHook: Big muscles, small testes, breast tissue, receding hairline, short fuse = anabolic steroids (testosterone).
